name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
خُذِ ٱلْعَفْوَ وَأْمُرْ بِٱلْعُرْفِ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ (١٩٩)
```

```english
Adopt [a policy of] excusing [the faults of people], bid what is right, and turn away from the ignorant. (199)
```

```arabic
وَإِمَّا يَنزَغَنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ نَزْغٌ فَٱسْتَعِذْ بِٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٢٠٠)
```

```english
Should a temptation from Satan disturb you, invoke the protection of Allah; indeed He is all-hearing, all-knowing. (200)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟ إِذَا مَسَّهُمْ طَـٰٓئِفٌ مِّنَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ تَذَكَّرُوا۟ فَإِذَا هُم مُّبْصِرُونَ (٢٠١)
```

```english
When those who are Godwary are touched by a visitation of Satan, they remember [Allah] and, behold, they perceive. (201)
```

```arabic
وَإِخْوَٰنُهُمْ يَمُدُّونَهُمْ فِى ٱلْغَىِّ ثُمَّ لَا يُقْصِرُونَ (٢٠٢)
```

```english
But their brethren, they draw them into error, and then they do not spare [any harm]. (202)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا لَمْ تَأْتِهِم بِـَٔايَةٍ قَالُوا۟ لَوْلَا ٱجْتَبَيْتَهَا ۚ قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أَتَّبِعُ مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ مِن رَّبِّى ۚ هَـٰذَا بَصَآئِرُ مِن رَّبِّكُمْ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٢٠٣)
```

```english
When you do not bring them a sign, they say, ‘Why do you not improvise one?’ Say, ‘I only follow what is revealed to me from my Lord; these are insights from your Lord, and a guidance and mercy for a people who have faith.’ (203)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا قُرِئَ ٱلْقُرْءَانُ فَٱسْتَمِعُوا۟ لَهُۥ وَأَنصِتُوا۟ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ (٢٠٤)
```

```english
When the Quran is recited, listen to it and be silent, maybe you will receive [Allah’s] mercy. (204)
```

```arabic
وَٱذْكُر رَّبَّكَ فِى نَفْسِكَ تَضَرُّعًا وَخِيفَةً وَدُونَ ٱلْجَهْرِ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ بِٱلْغُدُوِّ وَٱلْـَٔاصَالِ وَلَا تَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْغَـٰفِلِينَ (٢٠٥)
```

```english
And remember your Lord within your heart beseechingly and reverentially, without being loud, morning, and evening, and do not be among the heedless. (205)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ عِندَ رَبِّكَ لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبَادَتِهِۦ وَيُسَبِّحُونَهُۥ وَلَهُۥ يَسْجُدُونَ ۩ (٢٠٦)
```

```english
Indeed those who are [stationed] near your Lord do not disdain to worship Him. They glorify Him and prostrate to Him. (206)
```

## (Commentary)

These verses conclude the Surah, returning to the primary purpose for which the Surah was revealed. They command the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to adopt a beautiful and virtuous conduct that draws hearts and brings tranquility to souls, and command him first to remember (التذكر) and finally to engage in remembrance (الذكر).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **خُذِ الْعَفْوَ وَأْمُرْ بِالْعُرْفِ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْجاهِلِينَ** — "Adopt [a strategy of] forbearance, and bid to what is right, and disregard the ignorant." (7:199). To "take" (الأخذ) something means to adhere to it or not to abandon it. Thus, "adopting forbearance" (أخذ العفو) means consistently concealing the ill-treatment of those who wrong one and overlooking the right to retaliation that social reason (العقل الاجتماعي) grants to individuals against one another. This applies to the wrongs of others against oneself and the infringement upon one's personal rights. As for cases where the rights of others are violated through ill-treatment, it is not permissible to grant pardon, for that would be an incitement to sin (إغراء بالإثم), a more severe violation of the rights of others, and an annulment of the laws (النواميس) that preserve society. This is prohibited by all the verses that forbid injustice (الظلم), corruption (الإفساد), aiding wrongdoers, and inclining toward them—indeed, by all the verses that establish the foundations of divine laws (الشرائع) and legal codes (القوانين), as is evident.

Therefore, what is meant by His saying, "Adopt forbearance" (خُذِ الْعَفْوَ), is to cover with pardon that which pertains to his own person (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). This was indeed his conduct; it has been mentioned in some previous narrations regarding his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) character that he never sought revenge for himself against anyone.

This is based on what the commentators have mentioned—that "forbearance" (العفو) here is synonymous with forgiveness (المغفرة). However, in some subsequent narrations from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام), it is stated that it refers to the "middle path" (الوسط). This is more fitting for the verse and more comprehensive in meaning, avoiding the slight redundancy that would result from His saying, "and disregard the ignorant" (وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْجاهِلِينَ), according to the first interpretation.

His saying, "and bid to what is right" (وَأْمُرْ بِالْعُرْفِ): "The right" (العرف) refers to those beautiful customs and conducts recognized by the rational members of society and practiced among them, as opposed to those rare and deviant acts rejected by society and social reason. It is well known that commanding the following of "the right" necessitates that the commander himself follows what he commands. Furthermore, the command itself must be given in a "right" (معروف) manner, not a "wrong" (منكر) one. Thus, the requirement of His saying, "and bid to what is right," is to command every good deed and to ensure that the act of commanding itself is not done in an objectionable manner.

His saying, "and disregard the ignorant" (وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْجاهِلِينَ), is another command to practice forbearance (المداراة) with them. This is the nearest and most beautiful way to nullify the results of their ignorance and reduce the corruption of their actions. Indeed, meeting an ignorant person with an equivalent level of ignorance only incites them further and encourages them to persist in error and misguidance.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِمَّا يَنْزَغَنَّكَ مِنَ الشَّيْطانِ نَزْغٌ فَاسْتَعِذْ بِاللهِ إِنَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ** — "Should a temptation from Satan disturb you, then seek refuge in Allah; indeed He is all-hearing, all-knowing." (7:200). Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "The provocation (النزغ) is to enter into a matter for the purpose of corrupting it." He cited: **مِنْ بَعْدِ أَنْ نَزَغَ الشَّيْطانُ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَ إِخْوَتِي** — "...after Satan had caused a rift between me and my brothers" (12:100). It has also been said that it means agitation and incitement, occurring most often in a state of anger. Others have said it refers to the slightest whispering (الوسوسة) from Satan. These meanings are close to one another, and the most appropriate for the verse is the middle one, due to its suitability with the preceding verse commanding disregard for the ignorant. Indeed, their contact with a person through ignorance is a type of intervention by Satan to stir up anger and drive the person toward a similar ignorance.

Thus, the meaning of the verse returns to this: if Satan should provoke you through their actions based on ignorance and their ill-treatment of you—aiming thereby to drive you toward anger and revenge—then seek refuge in Allah; indeed, He is All-Hearing, All-Knowing. Despite this, the verse is general; it is addressed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) while intending his community, due to his infallibility (العصمة).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا إِذا مَسَّهُمْ طائِفٌ مِنَ الشَّيْطانِ تَذَكَّرُوا فَإِذا هُمْ مُبْصِرُونَ** — "When those who are God-conscious are touched by a visitation from Satan, they remember [Allah] and then they become insightful." (7:201). This serves as a sort of justification for the command in the previous verse. "The visitation" (الطائف) from Satan is that which circles around the heart to cast whispering into it, or it is the whispering itself that circles the heart to fall into it and settle upon it. The word "from" (مِنَ) is explanatory (بيانية) according to the first view, and indicative of origin (نشوئية) according to the second; the ultimate meaning of both is the same. "Remembrance" (التذكر) is a person's reflection on matters to guide them to a result that was previously overlooked or unknown.

The verse is like a justification for the command to seek refuge in the previous verse. The meaning is: seek refuge in Allah during Satan's provocation, for this is the way of the God-conscious (المتقين). When a visitation from Satan touches them, they remember that Allah is their Lord Who possesses them and nurtures them, and to Whom their affairs return. So, they return the matter to Him, and He suffices them of their burden, repels his plot from them, and lifts the veil of heedlessness (الغفلة) from them; then they become insightful (مبصرون), their vision no longer struck by the veil of heedlessness.

The verse—as you have seen—carries the same meaning as His saying: **إِنَّهُ لَيْسَ لَهُ سُلْطانٌ عَلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَلى رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ** — "Indeed he has no authority over those who have faith and put their trust in their Lord." (16:99). It has also become clear that seeking refuge in Allah is a type of remembrance (تذكر), because it is based on the fact that Allah, the Glorified—Who is his Lord—is the only pillar that repels this attacking enemy with His power. Also, seeking refuge is a type of trust (التوكل), as previously mentioned.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِخْوانُهُمْ يَمُدُّونَهُمْ فِي الغَيِّ ثُمَّ لا يُقْصِرُونَ** — "But their brothers, they draw them into error and then they do not stint." (7:202). This sentence appears to be a circumstantial clause (حالية). "Their brothers" refers to the brothers of the polytheists, who are the devils, as in His saying: **إِنَّ الْمُبَذِّرِينَ كانُوا إِخْوانَ الشَّياطِينِ** — "Indeed the wasteful are brothers of the devils" (17:27). "Stinting" (الإقصار) means to desist and cease.

The meaning is: those who are God-conscious are in this state of remembrance and insight, while the brothers of the polytheists among the devils draw the polytheists into their error and assist them, then they do not cease their drawing and assistance, or the polytheists do not desist or cease from their error.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِذا لَمْ تَأْتِهِمْ بِآيَةٍ قالُوا لَوْ لا اجْتَبَيْتَها** — "When you do not bring them a sign, they say, ‘Why do you not choose one?’" (7:203) to the end of the verse. "Choosing" (الاجتباء) is the reflexive form of "collecting" (الجباية). Their saying, "Why do you not choose one?" is a statement made in the manner of sarcasm and mockery. The meaning, as provided by the context, is: when you bring them a sign, they deny it; and when you do not bring them a sign—as when there is a delay—they say: "Why do you not choose what you call a sign and collect it from here and there and bring it?" **قُلْ** — "Say," I have no authority in the matter, **إِنَّما أَتَّبِعُ ما يُوحى إِلَيَّ مِنْ رَبِّي هذا** — "I only follow what is revealed to me from my Lord. This" Quran is **بَصائِرُ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ** — "insight from your Lord" by which He intends to make you see, **وَهُدىً وَرَحْمَةٌ لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ** — "and a guidance and mercy for a people who have faith."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِذا قُرِئَ الْقُرْآنُ فَاسْتَمِعُوا لَهُ وَأَنْصِتُوا لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ** — "When the Quran is recited, listen to it and be silent, so that you may receive [His] mercy." (7:204). "Silence" (الإنصات) is to be quiet while listening. It has also been said it is listening while being quiet. It is said: "He silenced the speech" or "He was silent for it," meaning he listened quietly. "Another silenced him" and "The man was silent," meaning he became quiet. Thus, the meaning is: listen to the Quran and be quiet.

The verse, according to its indication, is general, even if it is said to have been revealed regarding the congregational prayer.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَاذْكُرْ رَبَّكَ فِي نَفْسِكَ تَضَرُّعاً وَخِيفَةً وَدُونَ الْجَهْرِ مِنَ الْقَوْلِ** — "And remember your Lord within your self, humbly and fearfully, and without raising your voice..." (7:205) to the end of the verse. He divided remembrance (الذكر) into that which is within the self and that which is below the level of loud speech, then commanded both types. As for loud speech (الجهر) in remembrance, it is turned away from—not because it is not remembrance, but because it conflicts with the etiquette of servitude (العبودية). This is supported by what has been narrated: that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was traveling with his companions in one of his battles; they entered a desolate valley on a dark night, and some of his companions began calling out the *Takbir* loudly. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) forbade him and said: "You are not calling upon one who is absent or far away."

"Supplication" (التضرع) comes from *al-dara'ah*, which is to plead with a kind of humility (الخشوع) and submissiveness (الخضوع). "Fearfully" (خيفة) is a form denoting a type of fear (الخوف). What is meant is a type of fear suitable for the presence of His sanctity, the Exalted. In "supplication" there is the meaning of inclining toward the one supplicated, desiring Him, and drawing near to Him. In "fear" there is the meaning of being wary of Him, awe (الرهبة), and distancing oneself. Thus, the requirement of describing remembrance as being out of "supplication and fear" is that it be an internal movement toward Him and away from Him—like one who loves something yet stands in awe of it, so he draws near due to his love and keeps back due to his awe. Although Allah, the Glorified, is pure good with no evil in Him—and the evil that touches us is only from ourselves—He is the Possessor of Majesty and Honor (ذو الجلال والإكرام). He has the Attributes of Beauty (أسماء الجمال) that call toward Him and attract everything to Him, and He has the Attributes of Majesty (أسماء الجلال) that overpower and repel everything from Him. Therefore, the right of His remembrance—and He is Allah to Whom belong all the Beautiful Names—is that it be in accordance with what the totality of His Attributes of Beauty and Majesty requires: that He be remembered in supplication and fear, in hope and awe.

His saying, **بِالْغُدُوِّ وَالْآصالِ** — "at morning and evening": its apparent meaning is that it is a qualification for His saying, "and without raising your voice," such that verbal remembrance is distributed between the mornings and the evenings. This corresponds to some of the daily obligatory prayers.

His saying, **وَلا تَكُنْ مِنَ الْغافِلِينَ** — "and do not be among the heedless": this is an emphasis on the command for remembrance at the beginning of the verse. The Exalted did not forbid the origin of heedlessness (الغفلة), but rather forbade entering the ranks of the heedless (الغافلين)—those characterized by heedlessness in whom this quality has become settled.

It becomes clear from this that the required and commanded remembrance is for a person to be mindful of his Lord time after time, and to hasten to it should a forgotten heedlessness occur to him, not allowing heedlessness to settle in his soul. The following verse contains an indication of this, as will come.

The sum of the verse is: the command to persist in the remembrance of Allah within the self, in supplication and fear, time after time, and to remember Him verbally without loud speech in the mornings and evenings.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبادَتِهِ وَيُسَبِّحُونَهُ وَلَهُ يَسْجُدُونَ** — "Indeed those who are with your Lord do not disdain His worship, and they glorify Him, and prostrate to Him." (7:206). The apparent context is that it is in the position of justifying the command in the previous verse. Thus, the meaning would be: remember your Lord in such-and-such a way, for those who are with your Lord are like that; i.e., remember your Lord in this way so that you may be among those who are with your Lord and not leave their ranks.

It becomes clear from this that the meaning of His saying, "those who are with your Lord" (الَّذِينَ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ), is not only the angels—as many commentators have interpreted—since there would be no meaning in saying: "remember your Lord in this way because the angels remember Him in this way." Rather, it refers to all those brought near (المقربين) to Him, the Exalted, as suggested by the term "with your Lord" (عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ), implying presence without absence.

It appears from the verse that nearness to Allah is only through His remembrance; through it, the veil between Him and His servant is lifted. Otherwise, all things are equal in their relation to Him, without any difference between them in terms of nearness, distance, or anything else.

His saying, "do not disdain His worship, and they glorify Him, and prostrate to Him": this contains three matters that characterize internal remembrance just as they characterize verbal remembrance. For the soul can be characterized by the state of not disdaining, by the state of hallowing Him (تنزيهه), and by the state of prostration (السجدة) and perfect humility to Him, just as verbal remembrance is characterized by them and they are intended by external action. Thus, glorification (التسبيح) and prostration (السجود) are not restricted to the limbs, such as the tongue and others, as indicated by His saying: **وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ** — "There is not a thing but celebrates His praise" (17:44), and His saying: **وَالنَّجْمُ وَالشَّجَرُ يَسْجُدانِ** — "And the herb and the tree prostrate" (55:6), and His saying: **وَلِلَّهِ يَسْجُدُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ** — "To Allah prostrates whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth" (16:49).

The description in this verse of the people as not disdaining, glorifying, and prostrating is lighter and easier than what is contained in His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ عِنْدَهُ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبادَتِهِ وَلا يَسْتَحْسِرُونَ يُسَبِّحُونَ اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهارَ لا يَفْتُرُونَ** — "And those who are with Him do not disdain His worship, nor do they grow weary. They glorify [Him] night and day, and they do not flag" (21:19-20), and His saying: **فَإِنِ اسْتَكْبَرُوا فَالَّذِينَ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ يُسَبِّحُونَ لَهُ بِاللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ وَهُمْ لا يَسْأَمُونَ** — "But if they are disdainful, those who are with your Lord glorify Him night and day, and they do not tire" (41:38). For these verses apparently indicate a continuity that is not interrupted by non-existence, nor mediated by anything contrary. The verse we are discussing only commands that which prevents heedlessness from becoming established in the soul, as you have seen.

Thus, this verse commands a level of remembrance that is below what is contained in the verses of Surah al-Anbiya and Surah Ha Mim al-Sajdah. And Allah knows best.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, narrated from al-Hasan ibn Ali ibn al-Nu'man, from his father, from someone who heard Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) saying: "Indeed, Allah disciplined His Messenger, so He said: 'O Muhammad—**خُذِ الْعَفْوَ وَأْمُرْ بِالْعُرْفِ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْجاهِلِينَ** (Adopt forbearance, and bid to what is right, and disregard the ignorant).' He said: 'Take from them what is apparent and what is easy, and forbearance (العفو) is the middle path (الوسط).'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Anas who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Indeed, the most noble of characters (مكارم الأخلاق) with Allah is that you pardon the one who wronged you, join the one who cut you off, and give to the one who deprived you.' Then the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited: **خُذِ الْعَفْوَ وَأْمُرْ بِالْعُرْفِ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْجاهِلِينَ**."

**I say:** There are many narrations with this meaning narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) through Sunni chains.

In the same source, Ibn Abi al-Dunya extracted in *Makarim al-Akhlaq* from Ibrahim ibn Adham who said: "When Allah revealed **خُذِ الْعَفْوَ وَأْمُرْ بِالْعُرْفِ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْجاهِلِينَ**, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'I have been commanded to take forbearance (العفو) from the characters of the people.'"

In the same source, Ibn Jarir extracted from Ibn Zayd who said: "When **خُذِ الْعَفْوَ وَأْمُرْ بِالْعُرْفِ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْجاهِلِينَ** was revealed, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'How, O Lord, when there is anger?' So there was revealed: **وَإِمَّا يَنْزَغَنَّكَ مِنَ الشَّيْطانِ نَزْغٌ** (Should a temptation from Satan disturb you...)."

**I say:** There is something [questionable] in the narration, but it can be explained by what we previously mentioned regarding the verse.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: regarding the verse, he said: "If anything from him [Satan] or any whispering (وسوسة) occurs in your heart, then seek refuge in Allah; indeed, He is All-Hearing, All-Knowing."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Jabir ibn Abdillah who said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) reciting: **إِذا مَسَّهُمْ طائِفٌ** (when a visitation touches them) with an *alif*."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Abu Basir from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **إِذا مَسَّهُمْ طائِفٌ مِنَ الشَّيْطانِ تَذَكَّرُوا فَإِذا هُمْ مُبْصِرُونَ**. He said: 'It is the servant who intends to commit a sin, then he remembers and restrains himself; that is His saying: **تَذَكَّرُوا فَإِذا هُمْ مُبْصِرُونَ**.'"

**I say:** It was narrated by al-Ayyashi from Abu Basir, Ali ibn Abi Hamzah, and Zayd ibn Abi Usamah from him (peace be upon him). The wording of the first two is: "It is the man who intends to commit a sin, then he remembers and leaves it." The wording of the last one is: "It is the sin that the servant intends, then he remembers and leaves it." There are other narrations with this meaning.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Ibn Abbas who said: "The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) prayed, and some people recited behind him, so there was revealed: **وَإِذا قُرِئَ الْقُرْآنُ فَاسْتَمِعُوا لَهُ وَأَنْصِتُوا**."

**I say:** There are several narrations regarding this from Sunni chains. In some of them: "They used to speak behind him while they were in prayer, so it was revealed." In some: "It was a youth from the Ansar," and in others: "a man."

In *al-Majma'*, after mentioning the view that the verse was revealed regarding congregational prayer behind the Imam: "And that has been narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him)."

In the same source: "It is narrated from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) that he said: 'Silence (الإنصات) for the Quran is obligatory in prayer and otherwise.'"

**I say:** Al-Ayyashi narrated it from Zurarah from him (peace be upon him), and at its end: "When the Quran is recited in your presence, it is obligatory upon you to be silent and listen."

In the same source, from Abdullah ibn Abi Ya'fur from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "I said to him: 'A man recites the Quran while I am in prayer—is it obligatory upon me to be silent and listen?' He said: 'Yes, when the Quran is recited, it is obligatory upon you to be silent and listen.'"

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Abu Kahmash from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Ibn al-Kawwa' recited behind Amir al-Mu'minin (peace be upon him): **لَئِنْ أَشْرَكْتَ لَيَحْبَطَنَّ عَمَلُكَ وَلَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخاسرِينَ** (If you ascribe partners [to Allah], your work will fail and you will surely be among the losers), so Amir al-Mu'minin (peace be upon him) remained silent."

**I say:** The narrations regarding cases other than the recitation of the Imam are interpreted as being recommended (الاستحباب), and the full discussion belongs to jurisprudence (الفقه).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi extracted from Umar ibn al-Khattab who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) came to me, and I recognized sadness in his face. He took hold of my beard and said: 'Indeed, we belong to Allah and to Him we shall return. Gabriel came to me just now and said: "Indeed, we belong to Allah and to Him we shall return."' I said: 'Yes, indeed we belong to Allah and to Him we shall return—but why is that, O Gabriel?' He said: 'Your community will be put to trial after you for a short period of time, not long.' I said: 'A trial of disbelief or a trial of misguidance?' He said: 'All of that will happen.' I said: 'And from where will that come, while I am leaving among them the Book of Allah?' He said: 'By the Book of Allah they will be led astray. The first of that will be from their reciters and their rulers—the rulers will deny the people their rights and not give them, so they will fight one another; and the reciters will follow the whims of the rulers—**فيمدونهم في الغي ثم لا يقصرون** (so they draw them into error and then they do not stint).' I said: 'O Gabriel, by what will those who are saved among them be saved?' He said: 'By restraint and patience—if they are given what is theirs, they take it, and if they are denied it, they leave it.'"

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: regarding the meaning of His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ** (Indeed those who are with your Lord), he said: "Meaning the Prophets, the Messengers, and the Imams."

Completed, and praise be to Allah.
